items.q: What do orthodox Christians believe about the nature of Jesus?
items.correct: He is fully God and fully human at the same time (the incarnation) — both natures united in one person, as defined at the Council of Chalcedon.
items.misconception: Jesus was half God and half human.
items.random_wrong: Jesus was a human who became God after his resurrection.
items.why: The 'half and half' model was historically rejected as heresy. Orthodox doctrine holds both natures fully and simultaneously — a deliberately paradoxical claim students often soften.

items.q: What does the Abrahamic covenant in Judaism involve?
items.correct: God's promise to Abraham of land, descendants and blessing, in return for faithfulness — the foundational covenant marking Jews as a covenant people with responsibilities, not privileges.
items.misconception: Being 'chosen' means Jews believe they are superior to other peoples.
items.random_wrong: The covenant means Jews must not interact with non-Jews.
items.why: Chosenness in Jewish theology means chosen for responsibility (following Torah, being a 'light to the nations'), not chosen for favour. Students misread it as ethnic supremacy, missing the obligation it imposes.

items.q: Is the Buddha a god in Buddhism?
items.correct: No — the Buddha (Siddhartha Gautama) is regarded as an enlightened human teacher who discovered the path to liberation. Theravada Buddhism is generally non-theistic; some Mahayana traditions develop more elaborate cosmologies.
items.misconception: Buddhists worship the Buddha as their god.
items.why: Statues and offerings look like worship to students, but classically the Buddha is venerated as teacher, not worshipped as creator god. The path he taught, not his divinity, is what matters in most Buddhist traditions.
It's 1948. “1918” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: 'There is no compulsion in religion' is from:
items.why: It's The Qur'an. “The Bible” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many articles does the Universal Declaration of Human Rights have?
items.why: Exact answer: 30. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
